column7: THE PLUCKED string instruments make sound when stretched strings vibrate
column9: A guitar is a plucked lute;Players change pitch by shortening the vibrating length of the string;String tension, length, diameter, and density all affect frequency;Resonator bodies help project the sound;Different cultures create sound using the same basic physics in different ways
column11: How do you play a guitar?;How do musicians create different notes on a guitar?;What makes one string sound higher or lower than another?

column7: Instruments can express identity and their materials, size, and shape affect how they sound
column9: The alphorn represents Swiss identity and the hardingfele reflects Norwegian cultural identity;Humans have been making instruments for tens of thousands of years;Instruments can be built from natural or synthesized materials;Design choices help create specific sounds;Some instrument designs are visually striking as well as acoustically useful
column11: Which instruments suggest a nation, local area, or personal identity?;How long have humans been making instruments?;Which instruments here have especially interesting designs?

column7: A resonator helps us hear sound more clearly by transferring vibrations to a larger object
column9: Students compare how a music box sounds with different surrounding materials;A hard container can make the music box sound louder;The activity sets up the later resonator discussion;The goal is to notice how materials around a sound source affect loudness
column11: What role does a resonator play in helping us hear sound from an instrument?;Which material makes the music box louder?;Does it matter where the box or foam is placed?

column7: Resonators make instruments sound louder and strongly shape timbre
column9: Resonance happens when vibrating energy is efficiently transferred to another object or air column;Resonators can be boxes, gourds, tubes, membranes, or other structures;Resonators change both loudness and tone color;If too much energy is transferred the sound can distort;Some African instruments intentionally include objects that create a desirable buzzing or distorted quality
column11: How do resonators work?;Why do resonators make instruments louder?;Why might musicians want a distorted sound?

column7: A stylophone works because it is both a musical instrument and an electrical circuit
column9: Students predict how the stylophone works before it is demonstrated;The activity connects the stylophone to basic circuit ideas;The energy stick activity shows that a complete circuit can produce light and sound;Conductive materials and human bodies can help complete a circuit;This activity prepares students for the electricity and magnetism gallery content
column11: How does a stylophone work?;What connects the stylus to the stylophone?;What materials conduct electricity?

column7: Electric guitar pickups and related devices convert motion in a magnetic field into electrical signals
column9: Acoustic guitars depend on mechanical energy and a resonator;Electric guitars typically rely on amplification rather than a hollow body;A vibrating steel string disturbs the magnetic field around a pickup;That disturbance generates electricity in the coil of wire;The same core principle is used in microphones and many other technologies
column11: How are acoustic and electric guitars different?;What role do magnets play in pickups?;Can you find the exhibit's oldest electric guitar?

column7: Electronic instruments generate sound electronically and can be shaped in ways acoustic instruments cannot
column9: Synthesizers use oscillators to create electrical vibrations;Waveforms can be filtered and processed before reaching the speaker;The theremin changes sound through the player's position near the antennas;Electronic instruments changed music across many genres;Digital synthesizers followed earlier analog designs
column11: How does a synthesizer work?;How does a theremin work?;How is a theremin played?

column7: Effects pedals change an instrument's sound by altering its electrical signal
column9: Pedals perform signal processing on the electric waveform;Musicians can shape timbre by filtering, amplifying, or modifying certain frequencies;Distortion happens when a circuit is pushed beyond what it can reproduce cleanly;Clipped waveforms create a fuzzy, gritty, or growling sound;New technology helped expand rock and roll and other modern styles
column11: How do pedals change the sound of an instrument?;What does it mean to distort something?;How do advancements in technology influence music?

column5: How Science Brings Music to Life
column7: All musical sound begins with vibrations that travel as waves
column9: Every instrument creates vibrations even though instruments look very different;Vibrations push air molecules into repeating patterns that form sound waves;Amplitude relates to loudness;Frequency relates to pitch;Wave behavior helps explain how instruments create many different sounds
column11: What do all instruments have in common?;How are vibrations related to waves?;What is the difference between amplitude and frequency?

column7: Instrument design determines both pitch possibilities and timbre
column9: Some instruments have many fixed pitches while others change pitch by shortening strings or air columns;Tube length affects pitch in wind instruments;Timbre is the quality that makes two sounds different even at the same pitch;Most real sounds contain multiple frequencies at once;Materials, shape, and construction influence which frequencies stand out
column11: How do instruments create different pitches?;What is timbre?;Why can two instruments sound different on the same note?

column7: Innovation and technology continually reshape musical instruments and musical styles
column9: Instruments can change because musicians want them louder, smaller, stronger, or more flexible;Electronic instruments became especially important in the twentieth century;Modern genres such as EDM rely on advanced technology;DJs and electronic musicians use synthesizers, mixers, and drum machines;What seems standard today was once a new invention
column11: How does technology shape music?;What modern instruments depend on new technology?;What might instruments look like in 100 years?

column7: Hearing happens when sound waves are transformed by the ear and interpreted by the brain
column9: Sound waves enter the ear and vibrate the eardrum;Middle ear bones pass those vibrations inward;The cochlea converts vibration into nerve signals;The brain interprets those signals as sound;Loud sounds can damage hearing over time
column11: How do we hear?;What parts of the ear are involved in turning vibrations into sound?;How can loud sound affect hearing?

column7: Instrument invention combines creativity, craft, and the spread of successful ideas
column9: Inventors and makers develop new instruments to solve problems or create new sounds;Adolphe Sax is a clear example with the saxophone;Successful inventions often depend on refinement by later makers and performers;European instrument history includes both famous inventors and master builders;New instruments can change ensembles and musical styles
column11: What makes an invention successful?;How do new instruments spread?;What famous instrument inventors do you know?

column7: Mechanical instruments are early examples of automated music making
column9: Music boxes, barrel organs, and similar instruments store musical instructions physically;Pins, holes, cylinders, or other mechanisms trigger notes automatically;These instruments show that music can be programmed long before computers;Mechanical systems translate stored patterns into motion and sound;They help connect music technology to ideas about automation and coding
column11: How do mechanical instruments work?;What is being programmed into a mechanical instrument?;How are these instruments like computers?

column7: This page compares two different ways European instruments create sound: vibrating air in wind instruments and vibrating strings in bowed instruments
column9: Flutes start sound when air is split at an edge;Brass instruments start sound when buzzing lips vibrate the air column;Changing tube length changes pitch in wind instruments;Bowed strings create sustained vibration through friction between bow and string;String length, thickness, tension, and resonator design all affect pitch and tone
column11: How do flutes and brass instruments differ in how they start vibration?;How do bowed strings differ from plucked strings?;What changes pitch in wind and string instruments?

column7: Powwows are intertribal gatherings celebrating music dance and community;Dancers wear regalia with important cultural meaning;Movement often happens in a circle called a round dance
column9: The large drum represents both the instrument and the group playing it;Regalia may include bells and rattles that create rhythm with the drum;Animal materials such as elk skin and buffalo hide are often used
column11: What do you see happening in the painting;What is the painting made on;What parts of the regalia might move and make sound

column7: Totem poles represent family crests and stories;Music dance and carving traditions are deeply connected;Many instruments are made from natural materials
column9: Rattles are percussion instruments shaken to make sound;Different materials change how rattles sound;Ravens symbolize power and connection to the spirit world
column11: What animals do you see on the totem pole;What animal might represent your family;How might different rattle materials change the sound

column7: Handbells have existed since ancient times and became popular in Europe in the 1600s;Handbells later spread to the United States;Groups of players form a handbell choir
column9: Bells can be rung struck or plucked;Most bells are made of brass while larger ones may use aluminum;Each player usually controls only a few notes requiring teamwork
column11: How do you move a handbell to make sound;What material do you think bells are made from;Why might musicians need to work together in a bell choir

column7: Blues music developed from African American spirituals work songs and storytelling traditions;Blues music often expresses emotion and personal experience;Blues influenced many later genres including rock and jazz
column9: Musicians bend or slide notes to create expressive sounds;Instruments like the diddley bow and slide guitar show creative design;Many early instruments used household or natural materials
column11: Can music sound happy or sad;How might a musician make an instrument sound like it is crying;What objects could you use to make a musical instrument

column7: Many Italian villages had civic bands that played opera music outdoors;Italy has many traditional instruments and regional music styles;Music and dance were important parts of local celebrations

column7: Bagpipes are found across many European cultures;Most bagpipes include a chanter drones and an air bag;They are often used for dance marching and celebration music
column9: Bagpipes may use materials such as wood leather cloth or metal;Scottish pipes are well known but many countries have their own styles;Some designs have changed little for centuries

column7: Lithuania Estonia and Latvia share many folk music traditions;Folk music helped preserve culture and identity;Traditional instruments were often made from local materials
